name: Figs

book_claims.mechanism: Naturally contain calcium in bioavailable form

supplementary_research.finding: Dried figs are among the richest plant sources of calcium (162mg per 100g dried) and provide meaningful potassium, magnesium, and iron for plant-based diets (Vinson et al., 2005).

Figs contain high levels of polyphenols (particularly gallic acid and chlorogenic acid) with antioxidant capacity comparable to or exceeding many common fruits (Vinson et al., 2005; Solomon et al., 2006).

Systematic review of fig (Ficus carica) pharmacological properties found evidence for anti-diabetic, hypolipidaemic, and hepatoprotective effects in animal and preliminary human studies (Mawa et al., 2013).

culinary_pairings.source: Classic Italian antipasto; fat aids mineral absorption
Roasted figs with honey; traditional dessert
Aged balsamic with roasted figs; acidity balances sweetness
Dried figs with yogurt for breakfast; calcium-rich combination
Spiced fig preparations; traditional pastry filling

seasonality: Fresh figs have two short seasons: early crop (breba) June-July, main crop August-October. Extremely perishable fresh (2-3 days). Dried figs available year-round and concentrate nutrients. Mission, Kadota, Brown Turkey, and Calimyrna are common varieties.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Among the richest plant calcium sources. Bioavailability estimated at ~30-40%, comparable to milk. Especially valuable for vegans.
Dried figs rival bananas for potassium content. Supports blood pressure regulation.
Very high in dried form. Mix of soluble and insoluble; the tiny seeds add bulk. Supports gut motility.
Antioxidant capacity comparable to many berries. Gallic acid is well-absorbed and anti-inflammatory.
Non-heme iron; absorption improved 2-6x when consumed with vitamin C (pair with citrus).

synergies.mechanism: Vitamin C dramatically improves absorption of fig's non-heme iron and calcium
